data.title: Least shorted S&P500 stocks in May
data.description:
Medtronic (MDT) was the least shorted S&P 500 (SP500) stock as a percentage of float in May, with 0.62% of its shares sold short.
 
The medical device maker recently raised its dividend payment, marking 48 consecutive years of growth in payouts and increasing its dividend yield further above 3%, more than double the benchmark index’s sub-1.5% yield. However, the Ireland-based med-tech set its fiscal 2026 earnings guidance below expectations despite posting a double beat with its Q4 FY25 financials.
 
Alphabet (GOOG)(GOOGL) had a short interest of 0.63% of its float in May, with shares rising around 11% in the past month. The tech giant touted its AI investments at its annual developer conference last month, stating that it has released more than a dozen AI models and 20 features since last year's conference.
 
Apple (AAPL) was the third least shorted S&P 500 stock in May, with a short interest representing 0.64% of the float. The iPhone maker’s shares dropped 5% in the past month, during which it reported fiscal second-quarter results that topped estimates and increased its return to shareholders, including a new $100B share buyback program.
 
Here are the top 20 least shorted S&P 500 stocks as a percentage of float, as of May:

data.title: Trump blasts Fed boss Powell as 'numbskull' over rate-cut resistance
data.description:
U.S. President Donald Trump on Thursday called Fed Chair Jerome Powell a "numbskull" for keeping interest rates on hold and not cutting. 

At the White House, Trump contended that reducing rates by two percentage points would result in $600B in annual savings for the U.S.

“We’re going to spend $600 billion a year, $600 billion because of one numbskull that sits here [saying] ‘I don’t see enough reason to cut the rates now,’” Trump said during a bill signing ceremony.

It's one thing if inflation was accelerating, in which case the Fed might need to raise rates, Trump said. But inflation is cooling, he added, “and I may have to force something.”

Trump's remarks come just a day after he called on the central bank to slash borrowing costs by a full percentage point, citing May's softer-than-expected CPI data.

Dear readers: We recognize that politics often intersects with the financial news of the day, so we invite you to click here to join the separate political discussion.

data.title: ProShares launches two new ETFs
data.description:
ETFs provider ProShares announced on Thursday the launch of two new exchange-traded funds, ProShares Ultra Top QQQ (NASDAQ:QQUP) and ProShares UltraShort Top QQQ (NASDAQ:QQDN).

QQUP targets 2x daily returns of the Nasdaq-100 Mega Index, while QQDN targets -2x daily returns of the same index.

data.title: Consumer income growth can help economy avoid recession- J.P. Morgan
data.description:
Consumer income growth should help the U.S. economy to avoid recession, J.P. Morgan said in its midyear economic outlook.

"Our 2025 year-ahead outlook built off two propositions: momentum in the business cycle at the turn of the year was solid, and policy risks were bidirectional," Michael Feroli, analyst at J.P. Morgan, said in a note. 

J.P. Morgan added that in the next six months, the solid momentum has continued, with stable growth in jobs and spending. "We have gotten more clarity on policy it has been a mixed bag, as expected, but we think tilting in a less growth-friendly way."

But policy has also given some growth-supportive offsets, including a boost in Social Security payments, which has lifted personal income, offsetting some of the higher tax burden of tariffs, Feroli added.

Feroli also said personal incomes should get some support from President Donald Trump's One Big Beautiful Bill making its way through Congress, which J.P. Morgan expects will be passed in modified form before the August recess.

"These offsets should limit the risk that higher tariffs drag the economy into recession. Even so, we view recession risks as still elevated and see about a one in three chance the economy slips into contraction in the coming four quarters," Feroli said.

The capital spending will be hurt by ongoing uncertainty about global trading rules, said J.P. Morgan, adding, "Finally, U.S. policy is weighing on the global growth outlook, which in turn will depress foreign demand for U.S. exports."

Feroli also believes there will be a slowdown in the number of monthly job growth, as recent benchmark revisions suggested that this slowing has been happening sooner than expected.

"For the remainder of the year, the inflation story is likely to be centered around tariffs. Through May, there is scant evidence that tariffs are already being felt in a big way in consumer prices," Feroli said. 

J.P. Morgan added the cooling inflation trends are likely to heat back up this summer, and the Federal Reserve is expected to wait until December for the next rate cut.

data.title: Teck Resources, Sumitomo at odds over copper supply deal, exposing cracks in pricing system
data.description:
Canada's Teck Resources (NYSE:TECK) and Japan's Sumitomo Metal Mining (OTCPK:SMMYY) are locked in a commercial dispute over a major copper supply deal, exposing cracks in a decades-old pricing system as the market endures a shortage, Bloomberg reported Thursday.

The two sides have failed to agree on the sales terms for concentrated copper ores that Teck (NYSE:TECK) will supply to Sumitomo’s (OTCPK:SMMYY) smelter in Japan this year, and the dispute centers on the value of treatment and refining charges that will be deducted from the price of Teck's concentrates to cover Sumitomo’s smelting costs, according to the report.

TC/RC fees have plummeted to record lows during the past year due to a surge in global smelting capacity that has created stiff competition for mined ores.

The global copper industry has long relied on a single benchmark TC/RC set by major miners and smelters, with Chilean miner Antofagasta's (OTC:ANFGF) deals providing the reference for this year, and shifts in the benchmark have major implications for smelters' margins, with TC/RCs typically accounting for a third of their incomes, Wood Mackenzie said.

Antofagasta (OTC:ANFGF) and Chinese smelters struck a supply deal for 2025 late last year that set TC/RCs at $21.25/ton of ore processed and 2.125 cents/lb of refined metal produced, which has been widely adopted as a benchmark.

But Antofagasta (OTC:ANFGF) later signed smaller contracts with some Japanese buyers carrying higher TC/RCs of ~$25/2.5 cents, causing Sumitomo (OTCPK:SMMYY) and some other smelters to resist using $21.25/2.125 as their contractual reference, creating strains in commercial negotiations and reigniting a debate about the fairness of the benchmark system.

ETFs: (CPER), (COPX), (OTC:JJCTF)

data.title: Fed rate cut expectations rise on soft inflation data, signs of labor market cooling
data.description:
Market participants on Thursday pushed up their expectations for Federal Reserve interest rate cuts this year, following two sets of benign inflation data this week and some signs of cooling in the labor market.

Since cutting interest rates by 100 basis points at the end of 2024, the Fed has stuck to a wait-and-see approach as U.S. President Donald Trump's tariffs have clouded economic growth expectations. Though inflation has trended in the right direction, continued strength in the labor market has also kept the Fed on hold.     

This week's softer-than-anticipated consumer price index (CPI) and producer price index (PPI) reports for May have shown little effects from tariffs.

"With (Wednesday’s) highly anticipated CPI print coming in below expectations on MoM and core readings for its fourth month in a row, inflation has proven to be tamer and more controlled than initially expected," Rick Rieder, CIO of global fixed income at BlackRock, said on X (formerly Twitter) yesterday.  

Meanwhile, last week's nonfarm payrolls report showed a M/M moderation in May and significant revisions to job growth for March and April. 

"While tariffs could still present upside risks to inflation, (Wednesday’s) print along with a solid, but moderating labor market (highlighted by last week’s payrolls), significantly increases the likelihood of Fed cuts this year, particularly if there is material slowing in employment," Rieder added.  

On Thursday, data from the U.S. Department of Labor showed claims for insured employment - also known as continuing claims - for the week ending May 31 hitting its highest level since November 2021. 

"Labor market cooling: Seasonally adjusted initial claims for unemployment benefits held at a seven-month high last week. Without a seasonal adjustment, claims were running ahead of last year but were below the 2023 level. Continuing claims are at a four-year high," Nick Timiraos, the Wall Street Journal's Fed watcher, said on X. 

The current federal funds rate stands at 4.25%-4.50%. According to the CME FedWatch tool, the odds of that rate falling to 3.50%-3.75% in December now stands at 28.8% from 24.8% a day ago, and the odds of that rate falling to 3.25%-3.50% has inched up to 5.7% from 3.9% the previous day.

The Fed is in its blackout period this week, ahead of its fourth monetary policy committee meeting of the year on June 17 and 18. Traders almost unanimously expect policymakers to take no action on rates.

"Changes to trade policy have ushered in upside risks to both unemployment and inflation. But, with current data showing activity remaining resilient, we expect the post-meeting statement and Chair Powell's press conference will convey that the Committee is still in no hurry to adjust policy," Wells Fargo's Jay Bryson said. 

"We believe the FOMC will need to see more pronounced softening in the labor market to begin cutting the fed funds rate again this year. We expect to see this softening in the coming months and look for 75 bps of easing by year end," Bryson added.

Thursday's soft PPI data sparked a rally in bonds, while equities fluctuated amid trade and geopolitical tensions. Here are some exchange-traded funds that track the benchmark S&P 500 index (SP500): (NYSEARCA:SPY), (NYSEARCA:VOO), (NYSEARCA:IVV), (NYSEARCA:RSP), (NYSEARCA:SSO), (NYSEARCA:UPRO), (NYSEARCA:SH), (NYSEARCA:SDS), and (NYSEARCA:SPXU).

data.title: AppLovin drops as Culper Research says it's short
data.description:
• AppLovin (NASDAQ:APP) shares fell 5% on Thursday after Culper Research said it's short shares of the technology firm.
  • “We are short AppLovin ... because we believe the Company has blatantly misrepresented the scope of both its Chinese shareholders and its Chinese operations, posing not only risks to shareholders, but national security,” Culper Research wrote in its report.
  • Culper added that it believes AppLovin has been backed by Chinese national Hao Tang through a variety of offshore shell companies, with him ultimately controlling at least 9.8% of Class A shares. 
  • AppLovin has repeatedly denied the claims of Chinese ownership and ties to China.
  • AppLovin did not immediately respond to a request for comment from Seeking Alpha.

data.title: Hooker Furnishings outlines $25M cost reduction target while advancing Vietnam warehouse strategy
data.description:
Earnings Call Insights: Hooker Furnishings Corporation (HOFT) Q1 2026

Management View

  • CEO Jeremy R. Hoff reported that "we continue to take significant and deliberate actions to stabilize the company, drive improved sales and deliver strong gross margins as we execute on our accelerated cost savings program." Hoff highlighted that the company achieved its eighth consecutive quarter of market share gains within Hooker's Legacy Brands and introduced new collections and the "Living Your Way" modular upholstery program, which had significant placements at the Spring High Point Market.

  • Hoff emphasized the expansion of a multipronged cost reduction strategy with a target of approximately $25 million in annualized savings by the next fiscal year, supported by the recent opening of a Vietnam warehouse facility aimed at reducing lead times from about 6 months to 4 to 6 weeks. "We see the Vietnam warehouse as a game changer and win-win for us and our customers," Hoff stated.

  • CFO C. Earl Armstrong reported, "Despite the decrease in net sales, we reduced our operating loss by $1.6 million or 31% to $3.6 million, reflecting the impact of cost reduction initiatives implemented in the second half of the prior fiscal year." Armstrong detailed operating expense reductions of $2.2 million and noted restructuring costs of $523,000, primarily in severance.

Outlook

  • Hoff stated, "In total, from the June 2024 start of our initiative, we anticipate reducing our total annual spend rate by approximately 25%. These savings alone will substantially improve profitability."

  • Armstrong projected, "In fiscal '26, Hooker expects to realize about $14 million in cost savings, net of offsets and special charges. By fiscal '27, Hooker expects to realize $25 million in net annualized savings through these phased initiatives, which should enhance profitability, operational efficiency and long-term order value."

  • Hoff outlined product and digital initiatives: "Key initiatives include the launch of our new Margaritaville licensing program, a best-in-class international warehouse that enables us to reduce domestic safety stock, preserve working capital and shortened lead times in our collective living whole home merchandising approach."

Financial Results

  • Armstrong reported consolidated net sales of $85.3 million for Q1 2026, a decrease of $8.3 million or 8.8% compared to the same period last year.

  • The net loss for the quarter was $3.1 million or $0.29 per diluted share, compared to the prior year's net loss of $4.1 million or $0.39 per diluted share. Gross margins improved by 190 basis points.

  • Armstrong highlighted that the company reduced operating expenses by $2.2 million, with Hooker Branded achieving breakeven, and Domestic Upholstery and Home Meridian reducing operating losses by 55% and 17%, respectively. Cash and cash equivalents stood at $18 million, with inventory levels at about $64 million at quarter end.

  • Armstrong noted, "Subsequent to the end of our first quarter, we paid down all outstanding borrowings on our revolving credit facility. As of yesterday, we had approximately $3 million in cash on hand with about $63 million in available borrowing capacity net of standby letters of credit."

Q&A

  • Anthony Chester Lebiedzinski, Sidoti & Company: Asked about shipment cadence and the impact of tariffs. Hoff responded, "I can definitely tell you that the cadence changed pretty drastically for us with the tariffs. It definitely affects, what we call the mega customer, which is really the HMI customer more so than the many customers we have that are very different on the Hooker Branded and Domestic Upholstery side of our business."

  • Lebiedzinski questioned gross margin impacts from discounting at Hooker Branded. Armstrong replied, "No. We don't have that in front of us, Anthony."

  • Lebiedzinski asked about drivers behind higher May orders at Legacy brands. Hoff explained, "We significantly broadened our merchandising strategy with collected living and the things we talked about. And it's starting -- we believe it's starting to have a positive effect."

  • Lebiedzinski probed on HMI trends and Memorial Day performance. Hoff said, "We're still seeing significant uncertainty due to tariffs because there really hasn't been other than we know the 10%, but... until there's clarification, that definitely hurts." On Memorial Day, "The overall sentiment that we found was that, it was relatively positive for most retailers for Memorial Day."

  • David Joseph Storms, Stonegate: Asked about cost savings cadence for the year. Armstrong replied, "With Phase 2 and these new cost initiatives, we'd expect probably a $250,000 net impact, positive impact in Q2, probably the opposite of that in Q3... We expect really to hit pretty significantly in Q4 to the tune of about $3.5 million."

  • Storms inquired about capital allocation priorities. Hoff responded, "Strengthening the balance sheet right now is priority one. And you're right on dividends being very high priority for us... our #1 focus is to make that balance sheet as strong as possible."

  • Storms asked about seasonality and momentum. Hoff said, "I believe the second half will be stronger than the first half... that's a historical trend that I'm fairly confident in. I'm not as confident to say that our May trend will continue... I'm hoping, yes."

Sentiment Analysis

  • Analysts voiced cautious optimism, seeking clarity on margin impacts, cost savings pacing, and tariff uncertainties, with persistent questioning of sustainability and momentum.

  • Management expressed measured confidence in both prepared remarks and Q&A, frequently highlighting cost discipline and operational progress. Hoff repeatedly stated belief in the positive impact of merchandising and cost initiatives, with moments of hedging such as "I'm not as confident to say that our May trend will continue... I'm hoping, yes."

  • Compared to the previous quarter, analyst tone remained probing and cautious, while management's tone shifted slightly more confident regarding cost controls and strategic execution, though macro headwinds and tariff uncertainties were acknowledged as ongoing challenges.

Quarter-over-Quarter Comparison

  • Management increased the target cost savings initiative to $25 million in annualized savings by fiscal 2027, up from $18–$20 million discussed previously.

  • The Vietnam warehouse strategy advanced from planning to execution, with operational impacts now reported, including lead time reductions and positive customer feedback.

  • Financial results showed improved gross margins and a narrower net loss compared to the previous quarter, alongside a focus on cash preservation and debt reduction.

  • Guidance language reflected greater specificity and confidence in cost savings and operational streamlining, with continued caution on market conditions and tariff risks.

  • Analysts continued to focus on cost initiatives, margin sustainability, and macroeconomic headwinds, echoing themes from the prior quarter but with more specific questions on the pacing and impact of announced initiatives.

Risks and Concerns

  • Management cited persistent softness in the housing market, higher mortgage rates, and declining consumer sentiment as ongoing industry headwinds.

  • Tariff uncertainties, particularly regarding Vietnam, were highlighted as a key external risk, with Hoff stating, "Like everyone else, we are waiting to hear in July what the final tariff may be for Vietnam, where we source over 80% of our products."

  • Management outlined mitigation steps, including sharing tariff costs with source factories and implementing a 5% price increase, along with a phased cost reduction plan and supply chain improvements.

Final Takeaway

Hooker Furnishings management emphasized the company's ongoing transformation, highlighting accelerated cost reduction initiatives targeting $25 million in annualized savings, operational advances such as the Vietnam warehouse, and steady market share gains in core brands. While macroeconomic and tariff uncertainties continue to pressure demand, the company's leadership underscored its improved financial flexibility, operational streamlining, and readiness to capitalize on a market upturn, maintaining a disciplined focus on shareholder value and long-term growth.

Read the full Earnings Call Transcript

data.title: J&J touts phase 1 data for bleximenib for acute myeloid leukemia
data.description:
• Johnson & Johnson (NYSE:JNJ) said that new phase 1b data found that candidate bleximenib demonstrated antileukemic activity.
  • The selective menin inhibitor, in combination with Venclexta (venetoclax) and Vidaza (azacitidine), is under investigation for acute myeloid leukemia in individuals with certain genetic mutations.
  • The dose-finding study enrolled newly diagnosed, intensive chemo-ineligible AML, and relapsed or refractory AML patients.
  • J&J noted that bleximenib's safety profile was good, with no QTc prolongation signal, no events of Grade 3 or higher, and only three Grade 1 events at the recommended phase 2 dose.

data.title: U.S. Bank, Fiserv create integrated agent card issuance
data.description:
U.S. Bank (NYSE:USB) and Fiserv (NYSE:FI) formed a partnership that will integrate U.S. Bank's Elan credit card program into Fiserv's Credit Choice product with plans to introduce significant new capabilities later this year, the companies said on Thursday.

Credit Choice allows financial institutions to deliver a digital-first, branded agent-bank credit card program for consumer and small business card holders. As part of the alliance, Credit Choice, enhanced with Elan, will offer integrated digital technology that combines both debit and credit card account information for consumer and small business card holders within each financial institution's digital card management solution. 

As a result, customers can access credit and debit card information and servicing in one place. In addition, financial institutions will have easier access to the tools they need to onboard and service their cardholders, reducing friction and improving the ability to easily reach new potential card relationships, the companies said.

Users of the new platform will have access to Elan’s suite of agent-issuing capabilities. Fiserv’s (NYSE:FI) digital card solutions for consumer and business card and expense management will be integrated in the first half of 2026.

U.S. Bancorp (NYSE:USB) stock slipped 0.5%, and Fiserv (FI) stock dipped 0.6% in Thursday morning trading.
